stories.headline: Phil Weiser wins Democratic gubernatorial primary, defeating U.S. Sen. Michael Bennet
stories.summary:
Attorney General Phil Weiser defeated U.S. Sen. Michael Bennet in Tuesday's Democratic gubernatorial primary, securing the party's nomination to compete in the fall general election for Colorado's top office. Weiser's support in the Denver metro area and along the I-70 corridor from Eagle County west to the Utah border propelled his victory. With Gov. Jared Polis term-limited, the race represented the first open contest for governor since 2018.

On the Republican side, state Sen. Barb Kirkmeyer led with approximately 41% of the vote at 10:30 p.m., narrowly ahead of self-described high-risk humanitarian Victor Marx at 39%, with state Rep. Scott Bottoms of Colorado Springs in third with 20%. In the attorney general race, Secretary of State Jena Griswold led her Democratic opponents with 46% of the vote, ahead of Boulder County District Attorney Michael Dougherty at 20%.

In one of the night's most dramatic results, 29-year-old democratic socialist Melat Kiros defeated 15-term U.S. Rep. Diana DeGette in Colorado's 1st Congressional District primary, ousting one of the state's longest-serving members of Congress in what observers described as a significant rebuke of the Democratic establishment. Several other contested primaries for congressional and legislative seats were also decided across the state.

stories.headline: Update: Willow Fire west of Leadville grows; parts of town remain under pre-evacuation alert
stories.summary:
The Willow Fire burning west of Leadville continued to grow Monday, with portions of the nation's highest incorporated city — at more than 10,000 feet above sea level and home to approximately 2,600 residents — remaining under pre-evacuation alert. Gov. Jared Polis warned at a press conference that high winds in central Colorado would fuel the fire throughout the day and likely lead to a more expansive evacuation order.

The Willow Fire is one of several blazes burning simultaneously across Colorado as the state faces an unusually active fire season driven by dry conditions. Gov. Polis has declared a disaster emergency to marshal state resources for firefighting efforts across multiple active fire zones.

stories.headline: Trump administration's full-suppression wildfire policy under scrutiny after deaths of three Colorado firefighters
stories.summary:
The deaths of three wildland firefighters in Colorado's Snyder Fire are drawing national attention to the Trump administration's creation of the new U.S. Wildland Fire Service and its revival of a full-suppression policy for all fires under its management. The three firefighters — Emily Barker, 38, Sydney Watson, 27, and Nick Hutcherson, 27 — died Saturday after being overrun by flames in the remote Knowles Canyon area of Mesa County. Two other firefighters survived with burn injuries and were hospitalized in Denver.

Brian Fennessy, director of the newly created Interior Department Wildland Fire Service, defended the agency at a press conference Monday in Fruita alongside Gov. Polis, expressing confidence that the consolidated agency would produce a more unified and robust firefighting response. Critics, however, argue the consolidation of personnel from multiple Interior Department agencies has created confusion about command structures, and that the full-suppression mandate reverses decades of accepted fire management science.

Former federal firefighters and fire ecologists have questioned why crews were attacking the remote blaze, asking what structures or values were at risk that justified placing personnel in such dangerous terrain. Interior Secretary Doug Burgum's order mandating full suppression for every wildfire under the agency's management has been criticized as discarding a decades-long approach that used prescribed burns to reduce catastrophic fire risk. The Wildland Fire Service was established administratively without congressional approval, drawing personnel from the Bureau of Land Management, National Park Service, Bureau of Indian Affairs, U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, Office of Wildland Fire and Office of Aviation Services, but does not include the U.S. Forest Service.

stories.headline: Front Range fire departments deploy crews, equipment to battle Colorado wildfires
stories.summary:
Fire departments across the Denver metro area and Front Range have been mobilizing crews and specialized equipment to assist with the multiple wildfires burning across Colorado. Adams County Fire Rescue sent four crews and a Type 3 Brush Truck to the Ferris Fire in southwest Colorado, while Westminster dispatched four firefighters with similar equipment. West Metro Fire sent two sets of crews to the Snyder Fire in Mesa County, including a Rapid Extraction Module Support team trained in both wildland firefighting and technical rescue.

The Type 3 Brush Trucks deployed by local agencies are valued for their off-road capabilities in rugged terrain and can carry 500 gallons of water to remote locations. Westminster and Adams County officials are back-filling vacancies created by the deployments with other local staff to maintain coverage on the Front Range.

With the Fourth of July approaching, both Westminster and Adams County fire officials are urging Front Range residents to avoid consumer fireworks and attend professional shows instead. Officials cited extremely dry conditions across the region, warning that any small ignition has the potential to spread rapidly.

stories.headline: Denver City Council approves municipal sentencing reforms, reducing penalties for low-level offenses
stories.summary:
The Denver City Council voted 9-4 Monday to approve an ordinance updating the city's municipal sentencing requirements, both aligning them with a Colorado Supreme Court ruling and going further by reducing penalties for low-level, municipal-only offenses. The bill, sponsored by Councilwomen Shontel Lewis, Sarah Parady and Serena Gonzales-Gutierrez, reduces maximum penalties for municipal-only crimes without state counterparts — such as failure to leash a dog, violating curfew, smoking indoors and littering — to 10 days in jail and a $300 fine, down from 300 days in jail or a $999 fine.

The ordinance comes six months after the Colorado Supreme Court ruled that cities cannot impose sentences harsher than state penalties for the same offenses. The new law establishes a five-tier sentencing framework and creates a working group of approximately 20 members — including representatives of the mayor, city attorney, municipal public defender and Denver Police Department — to submit recommendations by March on further possible changes, including potential decriminalization of some offenses.

Mayor Mike Johnston, who initially opposed the bill, ultimately supported it after sponsors agreed to maintain higher sentences for more serious municipal-only offenses including threats to property, flourishing a weapon, indecent exposure and wrongs to minors. Council members Chris Hinds, Darrell Watson, Kevin Flynn and Amanda Sawyer voted against the measure, each saying they would have supported a version limited only to compliance with the Supreme Court ruling.

stories.headline: Xcel Energy franchise renewal advances through Denver City Council committee
stories.summary:
A proposed 20-year franchise agreement between Denver and Xcel Energy cleared the Denver City Council's Governance Committee Tuesday, advancing toward a full council vote ahead of an August 3 deadline for placement on the November 3 ballot. The deal would continue more than $180 million per year in franchise fees, infrastructure relocation savings and undergrounding funds flowing to Denver, and adds new requirements for shareholder-funded bill assistance and neighborhood-compatible substation design.

The current agreement, in place since 2007, has saved Denver taxpayers an estimated $3 billion by requiring Xcel to cover the cost of relocating its facilities during city public works projects. The new agreement would continue that obligation at an estimated $150 million annually, lock in a 3% franchise fee projected at approximately $34 million per year to the General Fund, and dedicate about $10 million annually for undergrounding overhead power lines.

New provisions in the companion Energy Partnership Agreement include a $2.5 million one-time shareholder-funded donation in 2027 for bill payment assistance, $125,000 annually for income-qualified affordability programs, and at least 1% of each substation construction budget dedicated to neighborhood-compatible design. A city-hosted dashboard would track metrics and spending, with five-year evaluations and a community advisory group overseeing the partnership.

stories.headline: United Airlines wins Denver zoning approval for 113-acre flight training campus near DIA
stories.summary:
Denver has approved rezoning of 113 acres near Denver International Airport to allow United Airlines to develop a major flight training facility, expanding the carrier's pilot training operations in the region. The project represents a significant investment in aviation infrastructure at one of the country's busiest airports.

Separately, Denver Mayor Mike Johnston has named Dave LaPorte as interim CEO of Denver International Airport, following the announced August retirement of Phil Washington. The mayor's office said the city will conduct a nationwide search for a permanent CEO, with community input incorporated during the final stage, and the appointment will require confirmation by the Denver City Council.

stories.headline: Coors family development company to open The Pottery in Golden next month, anchoring new Clayworks District
stories.summary:
AC Development, a Coors family company, is preparing to open The Pottery, the first building of the planned Clayworks District, a six-block mixed-use redevelopment of downtown Golden's northeast corner. The four-story building, which retains the historic brick façade and signage of Coors' original ceramics operations dating to the 1880s, will serve as the global headquarters for CoorsTek — a privately held manufacturer of customized ceramics for defense and semiconductor applications with annual revenues exceeding $1 billion — with move-ins expected to begin in August.

Sweet Bloom Coffee Roasters has been announced as the first retail tenant, planning to open this October in a 2,367-square-foot space on the ground floor. The full Clayworks District is planned as a 10-year buildout across six blocks, connecting Washington Street to the site of the former Coors ceramics plant between Clear Creek and the CO-58 Golden Freeway.

Darden Coors, fifth-generation descendant of brewery founder Adolph Coors and CEO of AC Development, said the company sees competitive advantages in Golden's walkable downtown as it tries to attract tenants amid challenging office market conditions along the Front Range. Approximately 65,000 square feet of office space in The Pottery remains available for lease, with interest reported from tech, aerospace and financial tenants.

stories.headline: Broncos announce 14 open training camp practices beginning July 31
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos announced Tuesday that 14 of their 2026 training camp practices will be open to the public, beginning Friday, July 31, and running through Aug. 19 at the team's new headquarters and training facility. Rookies are scheduled to report to coach Sean Payton's fourth training camp on July 22, with veterans arriving July 28. All open practices begin at 10 a.m., are free to attend but require a ticket, with availability through Ticketmaster starting Wednesday morning.

The club is increasing daily fan capacity from approximately 800 last summer to 1,000 this year by adding temporary bleachers on the north end of the practice fields. For the first time, the bleachers will be shaded. The former player parking lot is being repurposed for fan hospitality now that the new facility includes underground parking for players and staff.

Payton announced the team will not hold joint practices with other NFL clubs this summer, citing the Broncos' deep playoff run last season and limited offseason practice time as reasons to keep all on-field work focused exclusively on Denver's roster. The Broncos' preseason schedule includes a road game at Atlanta on Aug. 14 and a home game against Green Bay on Aug. 21.

stories.headline: Tim Hardaway Jr. leaves Nuggets, agrees to join Miami Heat in NBA free agency
stories.summary:
Tim Hardaway Jr., who had a standout season with the Denver Nuggets, agreed to join the Miami Heat in the first hour of NBA free agency, according to reports. The Heat are assembling a roster around Giannis Antetokounmpo and Bam Adebayo, with Hardaway providing perimeter shooting.

Hardaway's departure is one of several roster moves the Nuggets are navigating as the franchise manages an active offseason that also includes ongoing trade discussions around Aaron Gordon and contract extension eligibility for other key players.

stories.headline: Vietnamese family returns to Federal Boulevard with new restaurant Re Tre, reviving parents' 30-year legacy
stories.summary:
Sisters An, Thao and Thoa Nguyen are opening Re Tre (Rễ Tre), a new Vietnamese restaurant at 630 S. Federal Blvd. in Denver, in the same property where their parents ran New Saigon Restaurant for 30 years from 1987 to 2017. The restaurant, which translates to "bamboo root," opens Friday, July 3, and will serve brunch through dinner daily except Monday, featuring traditional Vietnamese cuisine including a daytime cafe and bakery focused on French pastries and Vietnamese-influenced coffee.

The Nguyen family represents a broad network of Denver restaurateurs; Re Tre follows the sisters' existing Dan Da restaurant and Banh & Butter Bakery Cafe in Aurora. The Federal Boulevard location is adjacent to New Saigon Bakery and Deli, run by another Nguyen sister. An Nguyen said the family aims to revitalize the Federal Boulevard dining corridor as they previously did on East Colfax.

An Nguyen, 42, acknowledged the challenges of opening a new restaurant amid rising food and labor costs in Denver, but said the family's connection to the location outweighed financial caution. The dinner menu will emphasize seafood and traditional fermented flavors, while brunch will focus on dishes reflecting what Vietnamese families eat in the morning.

stories.headline: Coloradans cross into Wyoming to buy fireworks banned at home as Fourth of July approaches
stories.summary:
With most consumer fireworks banned in Colorado, residents are traveling to fireworks retailers just across the Wyoming border on Interstate 25 to purchase products unavailable or illegal at home. On the Saturday before the Fourth of July holiday, approximately 75% of vehicles in the parking lot of a Cheyenne Phantom Fireworks location carried Colorado license plates, according to the store's Wyoming regional manager.

Colorado bans any firework that leaves the ground or explodes, limiting legal personal use to sparklers and fountains. Items such as firecrackers, Roman candles, bottle rockets, mortars and M-80s are prohibited for personal use in the state. Under active fire restrictions — though some customers noted recent rainfall had reduced their concern — even normally permitted fireworks are banned.

Fireworks retailers near the border stock the full range of products and direct Colorado customers with dedicated signage along I-25. Phantom Fireworks store manager Rachel Lejeune said July 3 is the single busiest day, sometimes reaching the store's fire-safety capacity of 400 customers. Front Range fire officials are separately urging residents to forgo consumer fireworks entirely this year and attend professional shows, citing dry conditions that could allow even small fires to spread rapidly.

joke.joke: Going to the gym at 6 AM just to prove to yourself you can wake up at 5:45 and immediately regret a decision.

topStory.headline: Homeland Security Agents Track Rochester Man to Home and Hotel After Harsh Email to ICE Director
topStory.summary:
Federal agents from Homeland Security Investigations visited the home of David Streever, 45, a Rochester, New York, resident, on June 23, while he was traveling in Finland with his daughter. The agents told Streever's wife they were investigating an email he may have sent threatening Todd Lyons, the former acting director of U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement. Hours after Streever landed at John F. Kennedy International Airport on Thursday evening, a third HSI special agent tracked him to the airport hotel where he was staying overnight and left a business card at the front desk.

Streever, a former journalist now working in the tech industry, told NPR he did not send any threat but acknowledged sending a strongly worded email to Lyons' government address in January, shortly after federal immigration officers fatally shot two people in Minneapolis. In that email, Streever warned Lyons that his conscience would torment him for his actions and compared him to a Nazi official. Streever said it was the only email he ever sent to Lyons. His wife, the Rev. Hilary Streever, an Episcopal priest, encountered the agents at their home while arriving with the couple's 2-year-old son and was handed a form reading "WARNING NOTICE" and "YOU MAY BE IN VIOLATION OF FEDERAL LAW" citing statutes criminalizing threats against federal officials.

Civil liberties advocates have criticized the Department of Homeland Security's use of these warning forms, characterizing them as an intimidation tactic intended to silence government critics rather than address genuine legal violations. On the same day agents visited the Streever home, they presented an identical form to a Syracuse poll worker whom they accused of threatening an ICE officer in an Instagram post. The pattern suggests a coordinated effort by HSI to confront individuals who have publicly criticized immigration enforcement.

The actions against Streever represent the latest in a series of moves by the Department of Homeland Security targeting protesters and critics over the past year. Free speech and civil liberties organizations have raised alarms that the use of warning notices and surveillance against private citizens exercising First Amendment rights sets a troubling precedent. It remains unclear whether federal authorities intend to pursue formal charges against Streever or others who received similar notices.
